title: Quiz: Wave-Particle Duality
description: Test your understanding of wave-particle duality and its role in quantum mechanics and computing

questions.question: In the photoelectric effect, what determines the energy of ejected electrons?
questions.explanation: Einstein showed that light consists of photons with energy E = hf. Each photon's energy depends on frequency, not intensity. Higher frequency means higher energy photons that can eject electrons with more kinetic energy.

questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is given by λ = h/p. If you double the velocity of an electron, what happens to its de Broglie wavelength?
questions.explanation: Since λ = h/mv, doubling velocity v doubles momentum p = mv. Since wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum (λ = h/p), doubling momentum halves the wavelength.

questions.question: In the double-slit experiment with electrons sent one at a time, what pattern builds up on the detector screen over many electrons?

options.text: A single band in the center
An interference pattern with multiple bands

questions.explanation: Even when sent one at a time, electrons build up an interference pattern! Each electron lands at a definite spot, but the probability distribution follows wave mechanics. This demonstrates that each electron somehow 'interferes with itself.'

questions.question: If we place detectors at the slits to determine which slit each electron passes through, the interference pattern on the screen remains unchanged.
questions.explanation: False! When we obtain 'which-path' information, the interference pattern disappears and we see the classical two-band pattern. The act of measurement fundamentally changes the outcome - this is one of the deepest mysteries of quantum mechanics.

questions.question: Why don't we observe quantum wave behavior in everyday objects like baseballs?

options.text: The de Broglie wavelength is immeasurably small
Quantum mechanics only applies to subatomic particles

questions.explanation: A baseball's de Broglie wavelength is about 10^-34 meters - roughly 10^19 times smaller than a proton! Quantum effects become observable when the de Broglie wavelength is comparable to the system size. For macroscopic objects, this never happens.

questions.question: How does wave-particle duality relate to quantum computing?

options.text: It has no relation to quantum computing
Wave-like behavior enables superposition; particle-like behavior appears in measurement
Quantum computers only use particle properties
Quantum computers only use wave properties

questions.explanation: Quantum computing exploits both aspects: the wave-like nature enables superposition (existing in multiple states) and interference (amplifying/canceling amplitudes). Measurement collapses the wave function to a definite particle-like outcome.

questions.question: What does Planck's constant h represent in the equations E = hf and λ = h/p?

options.text: The fundamental quantum of action linking energy/momentum to frequency/wavelength

questions.explanation: Planck's constant (h ≈ 6.626 × 10^-34 J·s) is the fundamental constant that bridges wave and particle properties. It sets the scale of quantum effects - the reason quantum behavior is only visible at atomic scales.

questions.question: Which of the following are experimental demonstrations of wave-particle duality? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a) Young's double-slit shows interference (wave property of light). b) Photoelectric effect shows light's particle nature (photons). c) Electron diffraction shows electrons' wave nature. d) Newton's laws are classical mechanics with no quantum content.
